sections.summary: Defines the scope as fresh pomegranates of commercial varieties (Punica granatum L.) supplied fresh after preparation and packaging, explicitly excluding pomegranates intended for industrial processing.

Sets minimum quality requirements for fresh pomegranates (e.g., whole, sound, clean, pest‑free, free of abnormal moisture, foreign smell/taste, frost/temperature/sunburn damage; appropriately ripe and able to withstand transport) and introduces classification into three classes.

Lists the minimum requirements for pomegranates: whole, sound (no rot), clean, free of pests, free of abnormal external moisture (except post‑cold storage condensation), no foreign smell/taste, free of frost or temperature damage and sunburn, and appropriately developed/ripened to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

sections.section: 2.1.1  The  pomegranates  must  have  reached  an  appropriate  degree  of  development  and  ripeness  in accordance with criteria proper to the varieties and to the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Requires pomegranates to have reached appropriate development and ripeness per variety and growing area, and be in condition to withstand transport/handling and arrive satisfactorily at destination.

sections.section: 2.1.1  The  pomegranates  must  have  reached  an  appropriate  degree  of  development  and  ripeness  in accordance with criteria proper to the varieties and to the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Requires pomegranates to have reached appropriate development and ripeness per variety and growing area, and be in condition to withstand transport/handling and arrive satisfactorily at destination.
States that pomegranates are classified into three classes, with definitions provided in subsequent sections.

Defines the 'Extra' class as pomegranates of superior quality, characteristic of the variety and essentially defect‑free except for very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

sections.section: Pomegranates in this class must be of superior quality.
sections.summary: States that pomegranates in the 'Extra' class must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping or presentation.

Defines Class I as pomegranates of good quality, characteristic of the variety, allowing slight defects in shape, colouring or skin (including slight cracking) provided these do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality, presentation, or the arils.

Defines Class II as pomegranates that meet the minimum requirements but do not qualify for higher classes, permitting defects in shape, colouring or skin (including cracking) so long as the arils are not affected and the fruit retain essential characteristics for quality, keeping and presentation.

Allows sizing by count, diameter (maximum equatorial diameter with an optional mm‑range table) or individual weight (with an optional gram‑range table), requires the package to be labelled when sized, and provides optional guide tables.
Permits tolerances for quality and size in each package for produce not meeting the stated class requirements.
Heading only: introduces the topic of quality tolerances but contains no detailed provisions in this entry.

Allows up to five percent (by number or weight) of pomegranates in the 'Extra' class package that do not meet the class requirements but conform to Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
Class I: up to 10% by number or weight of pomegranates may fail to meet Class I requirements provided they meet the requirements of Class II or, exceptionally, fall within the tolerances of Class II.

Class II: up to 10% by number or weight of pomegranates may fail to meet the class requirements or the minimum requirements, but this tolerance does not apply to produce affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption.
Size tolerances: for all classes, 10% by number or weight of pomegranates may be of the size immediately above and/or below that indicated on the package.

Provisions concerning presentation: packages must be uniform (same origin, variety, quality and size where sized; visible contents representative), pomegranates must be packed to protect the produce using new/clean/food‑grade materials (printing/labeling allowed only with non‑toxic ink/glue), and containers must meet quality/hygiene/ventilation/resistance standards and be free of foreign matter and smell, with packing in accordance with CAC/RCP 44‑1995.

Uniformity: each package must contain only pomegranates of the same origin, variety, quality and size (if sized); sales packages may contain mixed varieties/colors/sizes only if each variety is uniform in quality and origin, and the visible contents must represent the whole package.

Packaging: pomegranates must be packed to protect them, using new, clean, food‑grade materials that do not cause damage (printed materials allowed only with non‑toxic inks/glues), and packing must comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 44‑1995).

Description of containers: containers must meet required quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics for proper handling, shipping and preservation of pomegranates, and packages must be free of foreign matter and smell.

Marking or labelling: in addition to the General Standard for Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1‑1985), specific provisions apply for consumer and non‑retail packages covering nature of produce, identification, origin, commercial identification, net weight and optional official inspection marks, with particulars legibly and indelibly marked or shown in accompanying documents.
Consumer packages: consumer packaging must comply with CODEX STAN 1‑1985 and the additional specific labelling provisions set out in this standard.

Nature of produce (consumer packages): if pomegranates are not visible from the outside, each package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may show variety, class and size (if sized) expressed by count, size code and range, or size range.
Non‑retail containers: each package must bear specified particulars grouped on the same side, legibly and indelibly and visible from the outside or shown in accompanying shipment documents.
Identification: non‑retail packages must show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an identification code optional.
Nature of produce (non‑retail): name of the produce must be given if contents are not visible, and the variety should be indicated where appropriate.
Origin of produce: non‑retail packages must indicate country of origin and may optionally state the district or national/regional/local place name where grown.

Commercial identification: packages must state class and size (by count, size code and range, or size range) and may state net weight; footnotes allow use of food‑grade recycled packaging and require explicit packer/dispatcher identification where a code mark is used and national law requires the name and address.
Official Inspection Mark (optional): an official inspection mark may be applied to the package as an optional marking.
Contaminants: pomegranates covered by this Standard must comply with Codex maximum levels for contaminants and toxins and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

sections.section: 7.1 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193-1995).
sections.summary: Produce must comply with the maximum levels for contaminants and toxins set out in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193‑1995).

sections.section: 7.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Produce must comply with the Codex Alimentarius Commission's maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Hygiene: it is recommended pomegranates be prepared and handled in accordance with the relevant Codex Codes of Practice (General Principles of Food Hygiene, CAC/RCP 1‑1969; Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables, CAC/RCP 53‑2003, etc.), and should comply with any microbiological criteria established per CAC/GL 21‑1997.

sections.section: 8.1 It is recommended that the produce covered by the provisions of this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969), Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 53-2003), and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.
sections.summary: Recommendation that the produce covered by this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the Codex Code of Practice – General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 53-2003) and other relevant Codex hygienic or practice texts.

sections.section: 8.2 The  produce  should  comply  with  any  microbiological  criteria  established  in  accordance  with  the Principles for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria for Foods (CAC/GL 21-1997).
sections.summary: Obligation/recommendation that the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established under the Codex Principles for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria for Foods (CAC/GL 21-1997).
